ttps.evidence_quote: used brute-force attacks with common passwords to gain access to devices
targeted FortiGate management interfaces exposed to the internet by scanning for services running on ports 443, 8443, 10443, and 4443
mimikatz to conduct DCSync attacks against Windows domain controllers and extract NTLM password hashes
PowerShell script named "DecryptVeeamPasswords.ps1"
running port scans using the open-source gogo scanner
identifying SMB hosts and domain controllers
cyberattack took down many of its IT systems and blocked access to the Epic electronic medical records
granting them access to their Windows devices by launching the Quick Assist built-in remote control and screen-sharing tool
harvest the victim's credentials from the command line using PowerShell
deploys Black Basta ransomware using the Windows PsExec telnet-replacement tool
breached more than 500 organizations between April 2022 and May 2024, encrypting and stealing data
download of Qakbot, RMM tools like ScreenConnect and NetSupport Manager, and Cobalt Strike
credentials are immediately exfiltrated to the threat actor's server via a Secure Copy command
exploit these vulnerabilities by using specially-crafted Microsoft Office documents
an attacker would have to convince the victim to open the malicious file
trojanized versions that include a cryptominer downloader
launched using PowerShell via a modified start script
turns off Microsoft Defender protections by adding an exclusion
creates a Windows service named DrvSvc for persistence between reboots
loaded into a system process like dwm.exe using process hollowing
infected devices were likely first breached using a custom EternalBlue SMBv1 exploit that targeted internet-exposed computers
found the platform's shellcode injected in the WININIT.EXE process, a legitimate Windows OS process that handles the initialization of various subsystems
it downloads and executes additional files, such as PowerShell scripts, from legitimate hosting services like Bitbucket, GitHub, and GitLab
downloads and executes additional files, such as PowerShell scripts, from legitimate hosting services like Bitbucket, GitHub, and GitLab
a custom lightweight TOR network client to protect its network communications from interception
it executes scripts for creating scheduled tasks or modifying Windows Registry keys
it executes scripts for creating scheduled tasks or modifying Windows Registry keys
It achieves persistence using systemd services, an autostarting .desktop file, or by modifying various profile and startup files, such as /etc/rc*, profile, bashrc, or inittab files
Mines Monero while camouflaged as a "chrome.exe" process
spread to other Windows and Linux devices on the network using SSH and EternalBlue

The installer downloaded from the fake website is "enlisted_beta-v1.0.3.115.exe," which drops two executable files on the user's disk if launched, namely "ENLIST~1" (the actual game) and "enlisted" (the Python ransomware launcher)
The encryption uses the AES-256 algorithm, and all locked files receive the ".wncry" filename extension
it follows the common strategy of deleting the shadow copies from Windows to prevent easy data restoration

The attackers rely on CVE-2023-27350 to install Cobalt Strike, Meterpreter, Sliver, AnyDesk, and ConnectWise on target computers, using them to steal credentials and move laterally into compromised networks
the gang exploited CVE-2022-47986, a critical remote code execution flaw impacting the IBM Aspera Faspex file exchange product
The files are copied into a ZIP archive and later exfiltrated to Blacktail's servers
all encrypted files receive the ".buthi" extension
the MOVEit Transfer data-theft attacks, where a zero-day vulnerability was exploited to breach servers belonging to "hundreds of companies" and steal data
the threat actors were leveraging Log4Shell to breach corporate networks
a phishing document in these attacks, which used macros to fetch an EarlyRAT payload from a server associated with past Maui ransomware campaigns
upon launch, collects system information and sends it to the C2 server via a POST request
receives an ISO file containing "setup.exe" and "msi.dll," the first being the lure for the user to launch and the second being the malware dropper triggered by the executable
The executable pythonw.exe loads a modified obfuscated python310.dll that contains a Cobalt Strike beacon that connects to a command-and-control server address
created a persistence mechanism by making a run key named "Python" and the value "C:\Users\Public\Music\python\pythonw.exe"
PowerShell commands used for gathering user data, extracting ZIP files, and executing scripts
Findstr: command-line tool used for searching passwords within XML files
Python scripts used for executing the LaZagne password recovery tool and obtaining Veeam credentials
AnyDesk: legitimate remote management tool abused for maintaining persistence
KillAV BAT script used for disabling or bypassing antivirus and antimalware programs
PuTTY Secure Copy client used for exfiltrating the collected information from the breached system
AdFind: command-line tool used for retrieving Active Directory (AD) information
PsExec, BitsAdmin, and Curl, used for lateral movement
hackers leveraged the unauthenticated remote code execution (RCE) flaw to plant a web shell on the target's non-production NetScaler Application Delivery Controller (ADC) appliance
uploaded to the vulnerable appliance a TGZ archive with a generic web shell, a discovery script, and a setuid binary
They did SMB scanning on the subnet
used the web shell to check and exfiltrate Active Directory inventory
The attacker encrypted the discovery data using the OpenSSL library and readied it for exfiltration to a web-accessible location in compressed form as a tarball disguised as a PNG image
the hackers tried to cover their tracks by deleting the authorization file, which would prevent admins from logging in remotely
PowerShell scripts used by Rhysida operators terminate AV processes, delete shadow copies, and modify RDP configurations
PowerShell scripts used by Rhysida operators terminate AV processes, delete shadow copies, and modify RDP configurations
uses a 4096-bit RSA key with the ChaCha20 algorithm for file encryption
the attacker can brute force credentials using countless username and password combinations without being rate-limited or blocked for abuse
the attacker can establish a clientless SSL VPN session in the breached organization's network
CVE-2023-32315, is an authentication bypass impacting Openfire's administration console, allowing unauthenticated attackers to create new admin accounts on vulnerable servers
The attackers leveraged the flaw to create a new admin user on Openfire, logged in, and used it to install a malicious JAR plugin that can run arbitrary code
the attackers install malicious Java plugins (JAR files) that execute commands received via GET and POST HTTP requests

When executed, LostTrust will disable and stop numerous Windows services to ensure all files can be encrypted, including any services containing the Firebird, MSSQL, SQL, Exchange, wsbex, postgresql, BACKP, tomcat, SBS, and SharePoint strings
the encryptor will append the .losttrustencoded extension to encrypted file's names
Splashtop Streamer, Tactical RMM, PuTTy, AnyDesk, PDQ Deploy, Atera Agent remote administration tools for backdoor access
Open-source network tunneling utilities: Ligolo, Chisel
Lazagne and Mimikatz for harvesting credentials
FileZilla and Rclone for data exfiltration
the FBI observed the threat actors using custom PowerShell, web shells, and batch scripts to move laterally on the network, increase their privileges, and to disable security agents on the systems
the FBI observed the threat actors using custom PowerShell, web shells, and batch scripts to move laterally on the network
NetMonitor.exe, which poses as a legitimate process and "has the appearance of a legitimate network monitoring tool"
acts as a reverse proxy that enables the threat actors to remotely connect to the compromise network
a Chinese-backed threat group tracked as Storm-0062 (aka DarkShadow or Oro0lxy) has been exploiting the flaw as a zero-day since at least September 14, 2023
SRG would convince them to install a system management tool, which was later used to install other legitimate utilities that can also be used for malicious purposes
Rapid7 Managed Detection and Response (MDR) is observing exploitation of Atlassian Confluence in multiple customer environments, including for ransomware deployment

post-exploitation command execution to download a malicious payload hosted at 193.43.72[.]11 and/or 193.176.179[.]41, which, if successful, led to single-system Cerber ransomware deployment on the exploited Confluence server
The Lockbit ransomware attacks use publicly available exploits for the Citrix Bleed vulnerability (CVE-2023-4966) to breach the systems of large organizations, steal data, and encrypt files
hackers used HTTP GET requests to obtain Netscaler AAA session cookies after the multi-factor authentication stage (MFA)

Rhysida attackers have also been detected hacking into external-facing remote services (like VPNs that allow enterprise users to access company assets from external locations) using stolen credentials to establish initial access
exploiting Zerologon (CVE-2020-1472), a critical vulnerability enabling Windows privilege escalation within Microsoft's Netlogon Remote Protocol
CVE-2023-46604 is exploited to download and execute Kinsing cryptocurrency miners and malware on a vulnerable system
The malware uses the 'ProcessBuilder' method to execute malicious bash scripts and download additional payloads on the infected device from within newly created system-level processes
it establishes persistence via a cronjob that fetches the latest version of its infection script (bootstrap)
adds a rootkit into '/etc/ld.so.preload'
using a custom VSS Copying Tool helps steal files from shadow volume copies even when those files are in use by applications
Ransomware actors are again using TeamViewer to gain initial access to organization endpoints and attempt to deploy encryptors based on the leaked LockBit ransomware builder
the attackers attempted to deploy the ransomware payload using a DOS batch file (PP.bat) placed on the desktop, which executed a DLL file (payload) via a rundll32.exe command
a template injection weakness that allows unauthenticated remote attackers to execute code on vulnerable Confluence Data Center and Confluence Server endpoints
attackers are trying out callbacks by executing the 'whoami' command to gather information about the level of access and privileges on the system
Both Netwalker and Alpha ransomware use a similar PowerShell-based loader to deliver their payloads
Both delete themselves using a temporary batch file after the completion of the encryption process
recent Alpha attacks extensively employ living-off-the-land tools, including Taskkill, PsExec, Net.exe, and Reg.exe, for evasion

the Chinese Volt Typhoon hacking group used two FortiOS SSL VPN flaws (CVE-2022-42475 and CVE-2023-27997) to deploy the Coathanger custom remote access trojan (RAT) malware previously used to backdoor a military network of the Dutch Ministry of Defence
the JS file uses Windows installer (MSIEXEC) to run an MSI file from a WebDAV share, which contains the Latrodecturs DLL payload
Latrodectus performs various sandbox evasion checks before running on the device to avoid detection and analysis by security researchers
The downloaded JavaScript script is heavily obfuscated with comments that include a hidden function that extracts text from comments that start with '////'
it drops a DLL in the %AppData%\Custom_update folder named Update _b419643a.dll, which is then launched by rundll32.exe
The downloaded ZIP archives contain a Setup.exe executable, which is a renamed and legitimate executable for Python for Windows (pythonw.exe), and a malicious python311.dll file
it loads the malicious DLL, which extracts and executes an encrypted Python script
the threat actor attempt to exfiltrate data using the backup utility Restic
the installer launches an obfuscated .NET malware that contacts a Telegram or Mastodon channel to receive a valid download URL from where it will fetch additional components
the base64 payloads hosted on those platforms contain PowerShell commands that introduce a range of malware strains to the system, unpacked using 7Zip
The malware component 'Updater' registers tasks in the Windows Task Scheduler to ensure it persists between system reboots
AntiAV: Disrupts and disables security software by modifying its configuration files, preventing the software from operating correctly
3Proxy: Converts infected systems into proxy servers by opening port 3306 and injecting them into legitimate processes, allowing attackers to route malicious traffic
forensic evidence indicated that threat actors were able to access victim environments by leveraging compromised VPN credentials
the attackers perform "pass-the-hash" attacks on administrator accounts, which are used to establish RDP connections to Windows servers running Hyper-V
Fog operators disable Windows Defender to prevent notifications alerting the victim before the execution of the encrypter
deletes backups from object storage in Veeam and Windows volume shadow copies to prevent easy restoration
The ransomware encrypts VMDK files in Virtual Machine (VM) storage
The observed exploit tool leveraged the fact that the Windows file werkernel.sys uses a null security descriptor when creating registry keys

The tool exploits this to create a registry key (HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Image File Execution Options\WerFault.exe) and sets the "Debugger" value to its own executable pathname, allowing it to launch a shell with SYSTEM privileges
use Okta permissions associated with the compromised account to reach the victim company's cloud and SaaS applications
create new virtual machines on vSphere and Azure, using their admin privileges and configuring these VMs to disable security protections
disable Microsoft Defender and other telemetry features in Windows that allow them to deploy tools for lateral movement
deploy tools for lateral movement, such as Mimikatz and the IMPACKET framework
tunneling utilities (NGROK, RSOCX, and Localtonet) that allow access without the need of VPN or MFA verification
uses legitimate cloud syncing tools like Airbyte and Fivetran to move victim data to their cloud storage on reputable services like Google Cloud Platform (GCP) and Amazon Web Services (AWS)
targeting Active Directory Federated Services (ADFS) to extract certificates. Coupled with a Golden SAML attack, the actor could gain persistent access to cloud-based applications
prompts the visitor to install a "root certificate" by copying a PowerShell script into the Windows Clipboard and running it in a Windows PowerShell (Admin) console
These errors prompt the visitor to click a button to copy a PowerShell "fix" into the clipboard and then paste and run it in a Run: dialog or PowerShell prompt
'vim-cmd vmsvc/snapshot.removeall' for snapshot deletion, and 'esxcli vm process kill' for shutting down VMs
encrypts ESXi related files like '.vmdk,' '.vmx,' '.vmsn,' only partially (intermittent encryption) for faster performance
disables syslog and other critical services to hinder logging
using stolen credentials to log into the company's Citrix remote access service, which did not have multi-factor authentication enabled
PowerShell script contains a Base64 encoded script that will connect to a remote server to download another PowerShell script, which installs the Vidar information-stealing malware
contains a valid Registry file that adds a Windows autostart (RunOnce) entry that runs a PowerShell script
/bin/sh -c "for vmid in $(vim-cmd vmsvc/getallvms | grep -v Vmid | awk '{print $1}'); do vim-cmd vmsvc/power.off $vmid; done"
start encrypting files (e.g., VM disk, configuration, and metadata files), adding the .PLAY extension at the end of each file
CVE-2024-37085 allows attackers to add a new user to the 'ESX Admins' group... which will automatically be assigned full administrative privileges
the Windows certutil command-line utility to download SilentNight
PortYard tunneler... establishes connections to Black Basta's command and control (C2) infrastructure and proxies traffic
CogScan: A .NET reconnaissance tool used to gather a list of hosts available on the network and collect system information
Rclone tool to exfiltrate data
drops and exploits a vulnerable, legitimate driver to escalate privileges and disable active EDR processes and services
creates a new service for the driver, starts the service, and loads the driver
The initial intrusion was likely through the exploit of a recently patched PHP vulnerability (CVE-2024-4577)
use of DNS traffic to communicate with the command-and-control (C&C) server... DNS tunneling (a feature implemented based on the open-source dnscat2 tool)
the shell code also tries to look for SSH data in various directories and uses the info to attack known servers
sets up multiple cron jobs with randomized names and payloads execution frequencies
renaming the malicious services as '-bash' or '-java', to mimic legitimate processes and blend with normal operations
system logs are wiped to hide the signs of malicious activity
The attackers leverage WhatsUp Gold's legitimate Active Monitor PowerShell Script functionality to run multiple PowerShell scripts via NmPoller.exe, retrieved from remote URLs

the attackers use the legitimate Windows utility 'msiexec.exe' to install various remote access tools (RATs) through MSI packages, including Atera Agent, Radmin, SimpleHelp Remote Access, and Splashtop Remote
Iranian actors have used brute force, such as password spraying, and multifactor authentication (MFA) 'push bombing' to compromise user accounts
multifactor authentication (MFA) 'push bombing' to compromise user accounts and obtain access to organizations
the actors leveraged a compromised user's open registration for MFA to register the actor's own device to access the environment
Moving through the network was conducted via the Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP), sometimes deploying the necessary binaries using PowerShell opened through Microsoft Word
exploiting Microsoft's Netlogon (also known as "Zerologon") privilege escalation vulnerability (CVE-2020-1472)
adds an autorun key in the Windows Registry for persistence
terminates the following processes to free up critical data for encryption and disable security tools
Existing volume shadow copies are wiped to prevent easy system restoration
Windows Event Logs are cleared to hinder forensic analysis
CyberPanel 2.3.6... suffers from three distinct security problems that can result in an exploit allowing unauthenticated remote root access without authentication
the ransomware will create a unique AES key and IV and use them to encrypt the files on a server. Encrypted files will have the .psaux extension appended to the filename

Fog and Akira ransomware operators are increasingly breaching corporate networks through SonicWall VPN accounts, with the threat actors believed to be exploiting CVE-2024-40766, a critical SSL VPN access control flaw
the threat actors engaged in rapid encryption attacks targeting mainly virtual machines and their backups
the Windows encryptor will clear Windows event logs
the ransomware will append the .interlock extension to all encrypted file names, and create a ransom note in each folder
SteelFox creates a service that runs WinRing0.sys inside, a driver vulnerable to CVE-2020-14979 and CVE-2021-41285, which can be exploited to obtain privilege escalation to NT/SYSTEM level
activates the info-stealer component that extracts data from 13 web browsers
Lateral movement across the network was facilitated using tools like Windows Remote Management (WinRM) and PowerShell for remote control
executed scripts associated with the SystemBC malware and established covert channels, possibly for data exfiltration or command execution
uses the ChaCha20 stream cipher, an advanced and fast encryption algorithm, to encrypt files on the victim's system
scans the system for the presence of PowerShell and leverages it to delete its executable to evade identification and analysis
hackers gained access to a Remote Support SaaS API key that allowed them to reset passwords for local application accounts
the ransomware breach was caused by an employee who downloaded a malicious file onto a company device
the threat actors exploiting the vulnerabilities in the SimpleHelp RMM client to establish an unauthorized connection to a target endpoint
the attackers then proceeded to create a new administrator account named "sqladmin" to maintain access to the environment
installed a Cloudflare Tunnel disguised as Windows svchost.exe to maintain stealthy access and bypass security controls and firewalls
SMS phishing, voice phishing, and fake login pages that mimic corporate VPN products like Cisco AnyConnect, Palo Alto GlobalProtect, Fortinet, and Microsoft 365
they trick victims into installing RMM software like AnyDesk, TeamViewer, ScreenConnect, Atera, and Splashtop
PowerShell scripts to deploy infostealers, such as Stealc, Rhadamanthys, and Fickle Stealer, to steal data stored in web browsers
a custom PowerShell-based encryptor that encrypts files using AES and appends the ".crypted" extension, deleting original files
the threat actors breached Genea's network on January 31, 2025, through a Citrix server
the attackers exfiltrated 940.7GB of data from Genea's compromised systems to a DigitalOcean cloud server under their control
deployed AnyDesk, a legitimate remote access tool, and stole the company's data
used Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) to move laterally and expand their presence to as many systems as possible
launched the Linux encryptor on the webcam and used it to encrypt the network shares over SMB, effectively circumventing the EDR software
breached its PowerSource customer support portal through compromised credentials
triggers JavaScript that copies a malicious MSHTA command to the victim's clipboard, who is instructed to paste and execute it on the Windows terminal
convinced the employee to open Microsoft Quick Assist and grant remote access
QEMU was used to evade detection by routing network traffic through virtual machines created on the platform, which allowed persistent, yet undetected, access to the network
created a local admin account to connect via RDP
exfiltrated 868 GB of data to Backblaze cloud storage using the GoodSync tool
we also saw a threat actor leverage Exchange server vulnerabilities to gain target network access
deployed BlackCat ransomware payloads across the network via PsExec
spreads via infected USB devices to other devices on a target's network once deployed on a compromised system
DEV-0270 has been seen using setup.bat commands to enable BitLocker encryption, which leads to the hosts becoming inoperable
many of DEV-0270's attacks have exploited known vulnerabilities in Exchange (ProxyLogon) or Fortinet (CVE-2018-13379)
a hijacked Citrix web session indicating a successful MFA bypass attempt, session reuse across multiple IP addresses
sent them malicious Excel spreadsheets named "OKX Binance & Huobi VIP fee comparision.xls"
a second worksheet embedded in the file will download and parse a PNG file to extract a malicious DLL, an XOR-encoded backdoor, and a legitimate Windows executable later used to sideload the DLL
STONESTOP loads the POORTRY kernel-mode driver signed by Microsoft to terminate the associated protected processes or Windows services
targets are asked to paste a command disguised with a fake file path onto File Explorer, leading to the downloading of the PHP RAT from 'trycloudflare.com' and its execution on the system
add persistence via a Registry run key
A disk-swap attack occurs when the threat actors powers off a Domain Controller virtual machine (VM) and dettaches its virtual disk only to attach it to another, unmonitored VM they control
wiped of backup jobs, snapshots, and repositories
deliver and deploy ransomware binaries to encrypt all VM files detected in the datastores
spearphishing emails with attachments containing RAR files
create archives that extract executables into autorun paths, such as the Windows Startup folder
the command decodes a Base64-encoded URL and fetches a malicious Bash script from a remote server
uses 'xattr' (removes quarantine flag) and 'chmod' (makes binary executable) to bypass Gatekeeper
creates a Plist file (com.finder.helper.plist) and stores it in the user's LaunchDaemons directory, ensuring persistence via automatic execution on system startup
generated custom malware based on the Chisel tunneling tool to use for sensitive data exfiltration
the BlackCat operation is using the Impacket framework for credential duping and remote service execution to deploy the encryptor across an entire network
the encryptor embeds the Remcom hacking tool, which is a small remote shell that allows the encryptor to remotely execute commands on other devices on a network
breached Taiwanese multimedia software company CyberLink and trojanized one of its installers to push malware in a supply chain attack targeting potential victims worldwide
utilized a legitimate code signing certificate issued to CyberLink Corp. to sign the malicious executable
a custom PowerShell script was deployed to extract and decrypt stored MSSQL and PostgreSQL credentials, including DPAPI secrets

conducted a Bring-Your-Own-Vulnerable-Driver (BYOVD) attack by abusing Microsoft's legitimate consent.exe executable to sideload malicious DLLs that loaded vulnerable drivers (rwdrv.sys, churchill_driver.sys)
enumeration of Active Directory objects using tools such as dsquery, SharpShares, and BloodHound
created local admin accounts that were synced to Entra ID and used them to access the VMware vSphere console
disabled Defender real-time protection by modifying Active Directory GPOs and turned off behavior and file/program activity monitoring
execution of Impacket smbexec-style commands to run programs remotely
creation of scheduled tasks for batch scripts
a fileless PowerShell encryptor that generated random AES keys per run
CVE-2025-53770 being exploited to plant webshells that enable persistent access
exploited to plant webshells that enable persistent access
DLL side-loading a Go-based backdoor named Zingdoor
credential dumping via ProcDump, Minidump, and LsassDumper
Sting was executed through a Windows scheduled task named after the traditional Hungarian dish goulash
backdoored the systems with Supper malware and deployed the legitimate AnyDesk remote monitoring and MEGA data synchronization tools
moved laterally using Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) and the Windows Management Instrumentation Provider Host to deploy INC ransomware across the victim's network

Hooking the EtwEventWrite function to prevent data from being logged to the Windows Event Viewer
the threat actor accessed the endpoint and installed the Total Software Deployment Service, as well as a rogue instance of the ScreenConnect RMM

attackers are abusing it in vulnerable products via a maliciously crafted SAML message to gain admin-level access to the web management interface and download system configuration files
use the Mimikatz hacking tool to extract plaintext credentials from LSASS memory
move laterally with PsExec and the Impacket toolkit, executing commands via Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI)
modifying Group Policy Objects (GPOs) to deliver Warlock ransomware across compromised systems
the malware's JScript activates via Windows Script Host (WScript) from a temporary directory
establishes persistence by adding shortcut (.LNK) files to the Startup folder that point to a second JScript file
the package also includes a malicious version of a DLL (cryptbase.dll), which the application requires to function properly
system information is collected and exfiltrated to the attacker's VPS server over DNS (port 53)
trick company employees into installing Microsoft's Quick Assist tool

a ClickFix page instructing the target to run a PowerShell command on their system

The kernel driver is installed and registered as a fake OEM hardware service, establishing reboot-resistant persistence

DEV-1084 was then later observed leveraging highly privileged compromised credentials to perform en masse destruction of resources, including server farms, virtual machines, storage accounts, and virtual networks
Storm-0324 was observed distributing payloads using an open-source tool to send phishing lures through Microsoft Teams chats
The actor is known to distribute the JSSLoader malware, which facilitates access for the ransomware-as-a-service (RaaS) actor Sangria Tempest
Storm-0569 distributing BATLOADER through search engine optimization (SEO) poisoning with sites spoofing legitimate software downloads such as Zoom, Tableau, TeamViewer, and AnyDesk
Storm-0569 then uses PowerShell and batch scripts that lead to the download of BATLOADER
BATLOADER dropped a Cobalt Strike Beacon followed by data exfiltration using the Rclone data exfiltration tools and Black Basta ransomware deployment
Black Basta ransomware deployment by Storm-0506
Onyx Sleet exploited the TeamCity CVE-2023-42793 vulnerability as a part of a targeted attack
Microsoft Threat Intelligence identified a campaign attributed to Onyx Sleet that deployed a Sliver implant, an open-source C2 framework
The threat actor also performed a DCSync attack, a technique that abuses the Directory Replication Service (DRS) Remote Protocol to simulate the behavior of a domain controller
Storm-0501 created a backdoor using a maliciously added federated domain, enabling them to sign in as almost any user
the threat actor leveraged the Entra Connect Sync Directory Synchronization Account (DSA) to enumerate users, roles, and Azure resources
Lateral movement was facilitated using Evil-WinRM
BlackByte operators gained initial access by exploiting the ProxyShell vulnerabilities (CVE-2021-34473, CVE-2021-34523, CVE-2021-31207) present on the customer’s Microsoft Exchange server
the service MSExchangeMailboxReplication.exe writing an ASPX file to the folder

the adversaries installed the remote desktop application AnyDesk to access multiple systems
vssadmin resize shadowstorage command was used to resize shadow copy storage
BlackByte launched with a command-line parameter of -single with a SHA-256 hash
the adversary compressed local data from compromised endpoints and uploaded the archives to the anonymous file-sharing sites anonymfiles[.]com and file[.]io
an adversary leveraged the Kaseya Virtual System Administrator (VSA) agent agentmon.exe to launch the command processor and execute a variety of malicious actions
